text: Precious metal coordination complexes with $d^6$ valence electron configurations, such as Ru(II), Os(II), and Ir(III), serve as the foundation for modern lighting technologies, solar energy conversion, and photocatalysis. However, developing comparable complexes using abundant first-row transition metals has remained a significant challenge. While research in this area has historically centered on Fe(II), we demonstrate that isoelectronic Cr(0) complexes can achieve photoluminescence quantum yields and excited-state lifetimes that far exceed those of any previously reported first-row $d^6$ system. The metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) luminescence of these Cr(0) complexes is highly competitive with benchmark Os(II) polypyridines. Furthermore, we show that these MLCT states can be effectively harnessed for photoredox catalysis, enabling benchmark chemical reductions to proceed efficiently under low-energy red light. Our findings suggest that strategic molecular design can unlock the potential of abundant first-row $d^6$ metals, opening new frontiers in photophysics and sustainable chemistry.
